subject: english
title: PSLE English — Synthesis & Transformation Tricks (10 marks)

stats.headline: Synthesis = 10 marks. PSLE rotates the same 4 grammar patterns every year. Learn the keyword-to-pattern map and the most common traps — full marks become straightforward.

keyConcepts.title: Welcome — Synthesis & Transformation (10 marks)
keyConcepts.body: Synthesis asks you to REWRITE a sentence using a given keyword, keeping the meaning. 4 patterns cover the bulk of past PSLE questions. The hardest part isn't grammar — it's not changing meaning, tense, or adding info.
bullets: **A: Concession** — Although / Despite / In spite of
**B: Cause** — Because / Due to / Owing to / Since
**C: Condition** — If / Unless / Provided that
**D: Reported Speech** — direct ↔ indirect, with backshift

**The 3 rules of synthesis:**
① Don't change the meaning. ② Don't change the tense. ③ Don't add info.
Pattern **A: Concession** — Although / Despite / In spite of.
Pattern **B: Cause** — Because / Due to / Owing to / Since.
Pattern **C: Condition** — If / Unless / Provided that.
Pattern **D: Reported Speech** — direct ↔ indirect, with backshift.

keyConcepts.title: Pattern A — Concession (Although / Despite)
keyConcepts.body: Two contrasting ideas. The PSLE trap: students mix up the grammar after Although vs Despite.
bullets:
**Although + CLAUSE** (subject + verb)
✓ Although **he was tired**, he kept working.

**Despite / In spite of + NOUN PHRASE**
✓ Despite **his tiredness**, he kept working.
✗ Despite **he was tired**, he kept working.

**Conversion** (Although → Despite): verb → noun/-ing, drop the subject.
"Although it was raining" → "Despite the rain."

keyConcepts.title: Pattern B — Cause (Because / Due to)
keyConcepts.body: Same clause-vs-noun split as Concession. PSLE drills both because they share the conversion trick.
bullets:
**Because / Since / As + CLAUSE**
✓ Because **the bus broke down**, we were late.

**Due to / Owing to + NOUN PHRASE**
✓ Due to **the bus breakdown**, we were late.
✗ Due to **the bus broke down**, we were late.

**Conversion** (Because → Due to): same trick — verb → noun, drop subject.
"Because she was sick" → "Due to her illness."

interactiveQuiz.label: Concession — Although → Despite
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Although the weather was bad, the football match continued.
Rewrite using "Despite". Which is correct?
options: Despite the weather was bad, the football match continued.
Despite the bad weather, the football match continued.
Despite the football match, the weather was bad.
Despite of the bad weather, the football match continued.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Despite takes a NOUN PHRASE. "The weather was bad" (clause) becomes "the bad weather". Note: "Despite of" is always wrong — just "Despite".

interactiveQuiz.label: Cause — Because → Due to
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: Because the road was flooded, the bus could not pass.
Rewrite using "Due to". Which is correct?
options: Due to the road was flooded, the bus could not pass.
Due to the flooded road, the bus could not pass.
Due to that the road was flooded, the bus could not pass.
Due to flooding the road, the bus could not pass.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Due to + noun phrase. "The road was flooded" → "the flooded road". Option 4 changes the meaning ("flooding the road" implies someone is doing the flooding).

keyConcepts.title: Pattern C — Condition (If / Unless)
keyConcepts.body: Unless = "if not". When converting If → Unless, you must NEGATE the if-clause. Double negatives = the #1 trap.
bullets:
**If + present, will + verb**
✓ If you hurry, you will catch the bus.

**Unless = If…not** — flip the negation!
✓ Unless you hurry, you will miss the bus.   (= If you don't hurry…)
✗ Unless you don't hurry, you will catch the bus.   (double negative)

**Conversion** (If → Unless): drop "not" if there is one, add "not" if there isn't.
"If you don't study, you will fail" → "Unless you study, you will fail."

keyConcepts.title: Pattern D — Reported Speech (Direct ↔ Indirect)
keyConcepts.body: Convert direct speech (with quotes) to indirect (no quotes). Headline rule: BACKSHIFT — when the reporting verb is past (said / told / asked), every tense moves one step further back. The biggest trap: past → **past perfect (had + past participle)**.
bullets:
**Tense backshift table** (when reporting verb is past)
-- present (am/is/are) → past (was/were)
-- present continuous (am eating) → past continuous (was eating)
-- **past (broke / went) → past perfect (had broken / had gone)**  ⚠️ the past-past trap
-- present perfect (has done) → past perfect (had done)
-- will → would   ·   can → could   ·   may → might

**⚠️ The past-past trap** (most-missed)
Direct: "I **broke** the window," John said.
✗ John said that he **broke** the window.
✓ John said that he **had broken** the window.
Past + past = ambiguous time order. Use past perfect for the earlier event.

**Pronoun & time/place shifts**
-- I → he/she   ·   you → I/me   ·   we → they   ·   my → his/her
-- today → that day   ·   **yesterday → the day before**
-- tomorrow → the next day   ·   now → then
-- here → there   ·   this → that   ·   these → those

**Questions** — use `asked if / whether`; drop "do/did"; statement order; no question mark.
-- "Where do you live?" he asked. → He asked where I **lived**.
-- "Are you coming?" she asked. → She asked **if** I **was** coming.

**Commands** — use `told [person] to + verb`.
-- "Sit down!" the teacher said. → The teacher **told** us **to sit down**.

interactiveQuiz.label: Unless negation trap
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Original: If you don't wear a raincoat, you will get wet.
Rewrite starting with "Unless". Which is correct?
options: Unless you don't wear a raincoat, you will get wet.
Unless you wear a raincoat, you will not get wet.
Unless you wear a raincoat, you will get wet.
Unless you wear a raincoat, you will be wet.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: "Unless" already means "if not", so DROP the "don't" when converting. "If you don't wear" → "Unless you wear". The main clause stays the same — don't add a second negative.

interactiveQuiz.label: Past-past trap
interactiveQuiz.stem:
Direct: "I lost my wallet yesterday," Tom said.
Convert to reported speech. Which is correct?
options: Tom said that he lost his wallet yesterday.
Tom said that he had lost his wallet the day before.
Tom said that he has lost his wallet yesterday.
Tom said that he had lost his wallet yesterday.
interactiveQuiz.explanation: Two backshifts must happen together: the verb "lost" (simple past) → "had lost" (past perfect), AND the time word "yesterday" → "the day before". Option 1 misses both. Options 3 and 4 catch one but not the other.

keyConcepts.title: Start practising!
keyConcepts.body: Mixed practice drawn from PSLE 2016-2025 Synthesis & Transformation. Covers all 4 patterns from the slides. Trains the reflex of checking "same tense, same info, same meaning" on every rewrite.
bullets: **15 questions** — Concession, Cause, Condition, Reported Speech
Wrong answers auto-saved, reviewed in 1 week
AI marks word-for-word — partial credit if you almost got it but missed tense/info
You'll tackle **15 questions** — a mix across Concession, Cause, Condition, and Reported Speech.
Any Wrong answers are auto-saved, and reviewed in 1 week.
The AI marks your answers word-for-word — giving partial credit if you almost got it but missed the tense/info.

keyWords.definition: Two contrasting ideas joined: 'tired BUT kept going'. Keywords: Although + clause / Despite + noun phrase / In spite of + noun phrase. The grammar split (clause vs noun phrase) is the most common trap.
Same grammar split as concession. Because/Since/As take a clause (subject + verb). Due to / Owing to take a noun phrase. Convert verb → noun + drop subject.
Means 'if...not'. When converting If → Unless, NEGATE the if-clause (drop 'not' or add 'not'). Double negatives are the #1 trap.

In reported speech, when the reporting verb is past, every tense shifts one step further back: present → past, past → past perfect (had + past participle). Pronouns and time/place words shift too: yesterday → the day before, here → there.
① Don't change the tense. ② Don't add info. ③ Don't change the meaning. Every rewrite must pass all three checks before you submit.

subTopics.label: Concession (Although/Despite)
subTopics.description: Two contrasting ideas. Although + clause vs Despite/In spite of + noun phrase. Conversion trick: verb → noun/-ing form, drop subject. PSLE's most-tested pattern.

subTopics.label: Cause (Because/Due to)
subTopics.description: Same grammar split as concession. Because/Since/As + clause; Due to/Owing to + noun phrase. Same conversion technique applies.

subTopics.label: Condition (If/Unless)
subTopics.description: Unless = if + not. When converting If → Unless, you must NEGATE the if-clause. Double-negative trap is common.

subTopics.label: Reported Speech (backshift)
subTopics.description: Direct ↔ indirect. Backshift tenses one step (present → past, past → past perfect). Also shift pronouns and time words (yesterday → the day before). Past-past trap is the headline mistake.
